params.text:
This course introduces health researchers  and data professionals to the critical role of metadata in transforming raw data into meaningful, reusable scientific resources. Through a practical microlearning approach, learners will explore how metadata provides the essential context that makes datasets understandable, discoverable, and valuable beyond their original purpose.

The course explains how properly documented data can move from being isolated and underutilized to becoming a powerful asset for global collaboration. Learners will gain a clear understanding of how metadata supports the FAIR principles - making data Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable - and why this is essential for modern Open Science practices.

The course also introduces the DDI Codebook Standard, a structured metadata framework widely used to describe survey and health data. Participants will learn the difference between basic documentation and machine-readable metadata, and how structured metadata enables search engines, repositories, and other researchers to efficiently discover and reuse datasets.

By the end of this course, learners will be equipped with foundational and practical DDI Codebook  knowledge to improve the visibility, usability, and long-term impact of their health research data.

Contributors : DDI TWG,Dominic Nzundah (MEIRU)

keywords.main: 1.2 Why Health Data Needs a Metadata Standard

params.text: 1.3 Key Concepts
DATA
Primary information collected: numbers, text, observations.
METADATA
"Data about data" - structured description that makes data findable and reusable.
Findable Accessible Interoperable Reusable
FAIR Principles
Structured vs. Free-text
Structured metadata uses defined fields; machine-readable in XML/JSON.
Key takeaway: Your research paper describes your findings. Metadata describes your entire dataset - enabling secondary research, cross-study comparison, and data citations.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: ❌ Not quite. Structured metadata goes beyond summaries - it provides field-level, machine-readable descriptions of the entire dataset, enabling discoverability and reuse.

answers.text: It summarises the study's key findings in plain language.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! Structured metadata uses defined, machine-readable fields to describe the full dataset — not just what was reported. This makes data findable via search engines and reusable by other researchers.

answers.text: It provides machine-readable, field-structured descriptions of the entire dataset — including variables not reported in the paper — enabling discoverability and reuse.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: ❌ Not quite. It provides field-level, machine-readable descriptions of the entire dataset, enabling discoverability and reuse.

answers.text: It replaces the need for a data management plan.

answers.text: It ensures the research paper gets published in a journal.

5.Discovery & Reuse — Global researchers find, cite, and reuse the data. Impact grows
1.Study Conducted - Data collected but undocumented. Invisible to others
2.DDI Metadata Created - Study, file, and variable-level description using Metadata Editor
3.0 Metadata File Generated - Machine-readable DDI Codebook XML/JSON exported
4.Published on NADA - Catalog entry created; indexed by search engines

params.question: What format does the Metadata Editor export for publishing on NADA?

params.text:
3.1 Recommendations for Institutions

🤝 Collaborative Culture :  Appoint a Metadata Champion. Include PI, data manager, and field team in documentation. Hold a post-data-collection metadata workshop.

📋 Standard SOPs : Integrate DDI Codebook documentation into data management SOPs. Make structured metadata a funded deliverable in grant proposals.Download the MEIRU example. 

⏰ Start Early : Begin metadata at study design — not after data collection. Document variables as the questionnaire is built.

🌍 Publish & Share : Deposit metadata on NADA or another trusted catalog. Include data availability statement in all publications.
3.2  Knowledge Check

answers.text: After the final data analysis is complete.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: ❌ Not quite. Best practice is to start metadata at the study design phase. This preserves critical context and saves significant time compared to retrospective documentation.
✅ Correct! Metadata should begin at the study design phase — not after data collection. Starting early saves time, preserves contextual knowledge, and produces richer, more accurate documentation.

answers.text: At the study design and data collection planning phase — from day one.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: ❌ Not quite. Best practice is to start metadata at the study design phase. This preserves critical context and saves significant time compared to retrospective documentation.

answers.text: When the funder requests a data management report.

params.text:
3.3 Resources  and repositories  

  • 🎓 Official DDI Training Materials : Official DDI Alliance training slide decks and resources
  • 🎬 Introduction to DDI — CODATA/DDI Alliance Webinar : Video introduction to DDI Codebook and Lifecycle with tool demonstrations. Co-produced by CODATA and the DDI Alliance.
  • 💻 Metadata Editor : A multi-standard open-source metadata editor
  • 💻 NADA :  Microdata Cataloging Tool
  • MEIRU Data Repository
